items.description: in Java provides a set of methods for manipulating and working with 3D vectors. It has fields for x, y, and z components, as well as methods for adding, subtracting, multiplying, dividing, and normalizing vectors. Additionally, it provides methods for rotating vectors, lerping between two vectors, and calculating the magnitude of a vector.
items.name: Vector3f

items.description: calculates the Euclidean distance of a point from its origin, using the squared magnitudes of the coordinates and the square root operation.

returns.description: the square root of the sum of the squares of its input arguments.

items.name: length

items.description: computes the length of a point in 3D space by squaring its coordinates and summing them.

returns.description: a floating-point value representing the square of the length of a 3D object.

usage.code:
Vector3f myVector = new Vector3f(5, 6, 7);
float squaredLength = myVector.squareLength(); // returns the squared length of the vector as a float (i.e., 125)

items.name: squareLength

items.description: computes the maximum value of three arguments: `x`, `y`, and `z`. It returns the maximum value computed using `Math.max()` method.

returns.description: the maximum of the three input values, `x`, `y`, and `z`.

items.name: max

items.description: calculates the dot product of two vectors, returning a floating-point value representing the amount of linear correlation between them.

params.name: r
params.description:
3D vector that is to be dot-producted with the `x`, `y`, and `z` components of the function's output.

* `x`, `y`, and `z` are variables denoting the components of the vector in question.
* `getX()`, `getY()`, and `getZ()` are methods that retrieve the corresponding component values from the input vector `r`.

returns.description: a floating-point number representing the dot product of the input vector and the vector represented by the `x`, `y`, and `z` variables.

items.name: dot

items.description: computes the cross product of two vectors and returns the resulting vector.

params.name: r
params.description:
3D vector that the method `cross` operates on, and its values are used to compute the cross product of the two vectors.

* `getZ()`: Returns the z-component of the vector.
* `getY()`: Returns the y-component of the vector.
* `getX()`: Returns the x-component of the vector.

returns.description:
a vector with the cross product of the input vectors.

* The output is a new `Vector3f` object with the values `x_`, `y_`, and `z_` from the input parameters `r`.
* The values of `x_, y_, z_` represent the component-wise product of the two input vectors.
* The resulting vector has the same direction as the original vectors, but with the magnitude of their product.

items.name: cross

items.description: normalizes a 3D vector by dividing each component by the vector's length, resulting in a unitized vector representation.

returns.description:
a normalized vector with the same direction as the original vector but with a magnitude equal to the length of the original vector.

* The output is a `Vector3f` object representing a normalized version of the original vector.
* The magnitude (length) of the output is equal to 1, regardless of the magnitude of the original vector.
* The direction of the output is the same as that of the original vector, scaled by the ratio of the length of the original vector to its magnitude.

items.name: normalized

items.description: rotates a 3D vector by an angle around a given axis, returning the resulting vector.

params.name: axis
params.description:
3D rotation axis around which the object will be rotated.

* `axis`: A 3D vector representing the axis of rotation. It has three components - `x`, `y`, and `z`.
* `angle`: The angle of rotation in radians.

params.name: angle
params.description: 3D rotation angle of the vector, which is used to calculate the resulting rotated vector.

returns.description:
a rotated version of the input vector, taking into account the provided angle and axis of rotation.

The output is a `Vector3f` object representing the rotated vector.

The `axis` parameter represents the axis of rotation, and its dot product with itself is 1 - cos(angle), which is added to the original vector's dot product with the axis to produce the rotated vector.

The `angle` parameter represents the angle of rotation in radians.

The `sinAngle` and `cosAngle` variables are calculated using the sine and cosine functions, respectively, of the negative angle. These values are used in the calculation of the rotated vector.

usage.description: This method would create a vector `v1` with the values 1,2 and 3 in its respective axes. It then creates another vector `axis` with the values 4,5 and 6 in its respective axes. The method rotates the first vector 90 degrees around the axis vector by using the rotate() method.

items.name: rotate

items.description: takes a `Quaternion` object as input, rotates it by that amount, and returns the resultant vector in 3D space.

params.name: rotation
params.description:
3D rotation of the vector, which is applied to the original vector through the multiplication operation.

* Quaternion rotation is a mathematical representation of 3D rotations, consisting of a scalar component and a vector component.
* The conjugate of a quaternion is another quaternion that is the negative of the original quaternion.
* The multiplication of a quaternion by another quaternion results in a new quaternion representing the composition of the rotations.
* The properties of the resulting quaternion, such as its scalar component and vector component, can be accessed through methods like `getX()`, `getY()`, and `getZ()`.

returns.description:
a new `Vector3f` object containing the rotated position of the original vector.

* The output is a `Vector3f` object that represents the rotated position of the original vector.
* The x-coordinate of the output represents the cosine of the angle of rotation.
* The y-coordinate of the output represents the sine of the angle of rotation.
* The z-coordinate of the output represents the original position vector unrotated.

usage.description: In this example, the method rotate is called on a vector with three coordinates (5, 6, 7) and rotated by the quaternion rotation. The result of the rotation will be stored in the same vector object.

items.name: rotate

items.description: interpolates between two vectors, `dest` and `this`, based on a given factor `lerpFactor`. It returns the resulting vector by subtracting `this`, multiplying the difference by `lerpFactor`, and adding `this` back.

params.name: dest
params.description:
3D vector that the current vector will be blended with, using the specified `lerpFactor`.

* `dest`: A `Vector3f` instance representing the target position.
* `lerpFactor`: A floating-point value representing the interpolation factor between the current position and the target position.

params.name: lerpFactor
params.description: 0 to 1 factor by which the current vector is interpolated towards the destination vector.

returns.description:
a vector that interpolates between two given vectors, with the specified lerp factor.

* The `Vector3f` object represents a 3D vector with three elements representing x, y, and z components, respectively.
* The `dest` parameter is the starting point for the linear interpolation between the current position of the object and the desired destination.
* The `lerpFactor` parameter represents the interpolation factor between the current position and the destination, with higher values resulting in more interpolated movement.
* The function returns a new `Vector3f` object representing the interpolated position of the object.

usage.description: The code above creates two vectors v1 and v2 of type Vector3f with values (1, 2, 3) and (4, 5, 6), respectively. The lerp method is then called on vector v1 with vector v2 and the value of lerpFactor as arguments. The output should be a new vector containing the linearly interpolated values between v1 and v2 based on the value of lerpFactor.

items.name: lerp

items.description: takes a `Vector3f` argument and returns a new `Vector3f` object with the sum of the current instance's coordinates and those of the argument.

params.name: r
params.description:
3D vector to be added to the current vector.

* `x`: The integer value of the `x` component of `r`.
* `y`: The integer value of the `y` component of `r`.
* `z`: The integer value of the `z` component of `r`.

returns.description:
a new vector with the sum of the input vectors' coordinates.

The `Vector3f` object returned by the function represents the sum of the inputs `x`, `y`, and `z`. The resulting vector has the same properties as the original inputs, including its magnitude (length) and direction.

items.name: add

items.description: adds the components of a `Vector3f` object to the corresponding components of the current object, updating its position.

params.name: r
params.description:
3D vector that adds its components to the current position of the `Vector3f` instance being modified by the `addSelf()` method.

* `x`, `y`, and `z` are the components of the vector in the function.

The function performs the addition of these components to the corresponding properties of the current instance `this`.

items.name: addSelf

items.description: adds a scalar value to the components of a `Vector3f` object, returning a new `Vector3f` instance with the modified values.

params.name: r
params.description: 3D vector to be added to the current vector.

returns.description:
a new vector with the sum of the input `r` added to the corresponding components of the original vector.

* The `x`, `y`, and `z` fields represent the position of the vector in 3D space, respectively. Each field takes on values within the range of -1.0 to 1.0.
* The vector's magnitude (or length) is equal to the square root of the sum of the squares of its components: `Math.sqrt(x*x + y*y + z*z)`.

items.name: add

items.description: multiplies a vector by a scalar value and adds it to the current vector, resulting in a new vector with the combined values.

params.name: v
params.description:
3D vector that is to be added to the current vector, scaled by the specified `scale` value.

* `v`: A `Vector3f` object representing a 3D vector with x, y, and z components.
* `scale`: An integer value representing the scaling factor applied to the `v`.

params.name: scale
params.description: scalar value by which the `v` input is multiplied before being added to the current vector.

returns.description:
a new vector that is the result of adding the provided vector scaled by the given factor to the original vector.

The `Vector3f` object returned by the function represents the result of adding the provided `v` vector scaled by `scale`. Specifically, it is a new instance of `Vector3f` with the same x, y, and z components as the original `this` vector, but with the magnification applied to each component. Therefore, the resulting vector has the same direction and magnitude as the original `this` vector, but with the scaled value of the provided `v` vector added to it.

usage.description: This example uses a vector with components (2, 4, 6) and applies the scaling factor of 0.5 to it. The resulting vector with the added scaled vector is then printed.

items.name: addScaledVector

items.description: multiplies a `Vector3f` object by a floating-point value and adds the result to the current vector instance, scaling its components proportionally.

params.name: v
params.description:
3D vector to be scaled.

* `v`: A `Vector3f` object representing a 3D vector with x, y, and z components.
* `scale`: A floating-point value representing the scaling factor to be applied to `v`.

params.name: scale
params.description: 3D scaling factor applied to the vector being added to the object's position, rotation, and scale.

items.name: addSelfScaledVector

items.description: takes a `Vector3f` argument `r` and returns a new `Vector3f` instance representing the difference between the current instance and `r`.

params.name: r
params.description:
3D vector to be subtracted from the current vector.

* `x`: The x-coordinate of the input vector.
* `y`: The y-coordinate of the input vector.
* `z`: The z-coordinate of the input vector.

returns.description:
a new `Vector3f` instance representing the difference between the input vector and the reference vector.

* The Vector3f object represents the difference between the original vector's components and the reference vector's components.
* The resulting vector has the same unit as the original vector, which means that its magnitude is preserved.
* The direction of the resulting vector is the opposite of the reference vector's direction with respect to the original vector.

items.name: sub

items.description: takes a scalar `r` and returns a new `Vector3f` instance representing the vector subtraction of the input vector's components from the origin, where (x, y, z) = (0,0,0).

params.name: r
params.description: 3D vector to subtract from the current vector.

returns.description:
a new `Vector3f` instance representing the difference between the original vector and the given value.

The `Vector3f` object returned by the function has three components: `x`, `y`, and `z`. Each component represents the difference in value between the original position of the vector and the position after subtraction. For example, if the original position of the vector is `(3, 4, 5)`, and the subtraction is performed with a value of `2`, the resulting vector would be `(1, 2, 3)`.

items.name: sub

items.description: multiplies two `Vector3f` objects, returning a new vector with the product of their x, y, and z components.

params.name: r
params.description:
3D vector that the method multiplies the original vector by.

* `x`, `y`, and `z` represent the components of `r`.
* The components are scalars that can be multiplied element-wise with the corresponding components of `this` to produce new values for each component.

returns.description:
a new vector with the product of the input vectors' coordinates.

* The output is a new instance of the `Vector3f` class.
* The `x`, `y`, and `z` components of the output are calculated by multiplying the corresponding components of the input `r` vector by the scalars `x`, `y`, and `z`, respectively.
* The resulting vector has the same direction as the input vector, but its magnitude is increased by the product of the scalar multiplication.

items.name: mul

items.description: multiplies a vector by a scalar value, resulting in a new vector with the same components scaled by the given factor.

params.name: r
params.description: scalar value used to multiply the components of the `Vector3f` instance.

returns.description:
a new vector with the product of the input `r` and the corresponding components of the original vector.

The returned vector has three components, each representing the product of the corresponding component of the input vector with the scalar value `r`.

The magnitude (or length) of the output vector is equal to the product of the magnitudes of the input components and the scalar value `r`.

The direction of the output vector is unchanged from the input vector, as the multiplication is a component-wise operation.

items.name: mul

items.description: takes a reference to another `Vector3f` object and returns a new `Vector3f` object with the component values of the original divided by the corresponding values of the referenced object.

params.name: r
params.description:
vector to divide by, and it is used to calculate the output vector's components.

* `x`, `y`, and `z` are members of `Vector3f`.
* They denote the coordinates of the vector.

The function returns a new `Vector3f` object with scaled coordinates based on the divisor `r`.

returns.description:
a vector with the same components as the input vector, but with the values scaled by the inverse of the input vector's magnitude.

* The output is a new `Vector3f` instance with values x / r.getX(), y / r.getY(), and z / r.getZ().
* The output represents the result of dividing the input vector by the specified reference vector.
* The resulting vector has the same units as the input vector, representing a scaling of the input vector by the ratio of the reference vector's magnitude to its own magnitude.

items.name: div

items.description: takes a single float argument `r` and returns a new `Vector3f` instance with x, y, and z components scaled by the ratio of `r`.

params.name: r
params.description: scalar value that the function will divide each component of the `Vector3f` object by.

returns.description:
a vector with components scaled by the divisor `r`.

* The output is a new instance of the `Vector3f` class, representing the division of the input vector by the specified scalar value.
* The resulting vector has the same coordinates as the input vector, but with each coordinate divided by the scalar value.
* The scaled coordinates are stored in the same locations as the original input vector, with no change to their original values or attributes.

items.name: div

items.description: computes the absolute value of a vector's components and returns a new vector object with those values.

returns.description:
a new vector with the absolute value of the original vector's components.

The return type is `Vector3f`, indicating that the function returns a new instance of the `Vector3f` class with the absolute value of the input values.

The expression `Math.abs(x)` returns the absolute value of the `x` variable, which is a double value representing the x-coordinate of a 3D vector. Similarly, `Math.abs(y)` and `Math.abs(z)` return the absolute values of the y- and z-coordinates, respectively.

The resulting `Vector3f` object has the same coordinates as the input vector, but with all values replaced by their absolute values.

usage.code:
Vector3f vector = new Vector3f(-1, -2, -3);
System.out.println(vector.abs()); //Outputs (1, 2, 3)

items.name: abs

items.description: returns a string representation of an object by concatenating its component values.

returns.description:
a string representation of the object's state, including its `x`, `y`, and `z` components.

* The parentheses are included as part of the output to indicate that the value is a composite one consisting of three elements - x, y, and z.
* Each element is separated by a space from the others.
* There is no explicit indication of the data types of each element, as the `toString` function is designed to work with any type of object that can be converted to a string.

usage.code:
Vector3f vector = new Vector3f(2, 4, 6);
System.out.println(vector.toString()); // prints (2, 4, 6)
usage.description: This code creates a new Vector3f object with the coordinates 2, 4, and 6 and then uses the toString method to convert it into a string representation of the vector. The resulting output is "(2, 4, 6)", which is a string representation of the vector.

items.name: toString

items.description: returns a `Vector2f` object representing the position of an entity in a two-dimensional space, with its x and y components as attributes.

returns.description:
a `Vector2f` object containing the x and y coordinates of the point.

The Vector2f object represents a 2D location in homogeneous coordinates, with x representing the horizontal component and y representing the vertical component. The Vector2f class is a part of the Java 8 Math library. The returned vector has an x component of 0, which means it represents the origin (0, 0) on the coordinate plane. Similarly, the y component is also 0, indicating that the location is at the origin. Therefore, the returned Vector2f object represents the point (0, 0) in the Cartesian coordinate system.

items.name: getXY

items.description: returns a `Vector2f` object representing the y and z coordinates of a point.

returns.description:
a `Vector2f` object representing the position of the entity in the Y-Z plane.

* `y`: The y-component of the vector, representing the vertical position in the 2D space.
* `z`: The z-component of the vector, representing the horizontal position in the 2D space.

Both components are represented as floating-point values, allowing for precise calculations and manipulations within the 2D space.

items.name: getYZ

items.description: returns a `Vector2f` object containing the `z` and `x` coordinates of a point.

returns.description:
a `Vector2f` object representing the coordinates (x, z) of a point.

The Vector2f object returned has two components - z and x. The z component represents the vertical coordinate of the point, while the x component represents the horizontal coordinate.

usage.description: This would print:

items.name: getZX

items.description: returns a new `Vector2f` object representing the coordinates (x, y).

returns.description:
a `Vector2f` object containing the values of `y` and `x`.

* The output is a `Vector2f` object, which represents a point in 2D space with x and y coordinates.
* The x coordinate is represented by the variable `x`, while the y coordinate is represented by the variable `y`.
* Both `x` and `y` are of type `float`, indicating that they can hold decimal values.

items.name: getYX

items.description: returns a `Vector2f` instance representing the point with coordinates `z` and `y`.

returns.description:
a `Vector2f` object containing the `z` and `y` coordinates of the point.

* `z`: The x-coordinate of the vector.
* `y`: The y-coordinate of the vector.

The `getZY` function returns a new instance of the `Vector2f` class, which represents a two-dimensional vector in homogeneous coordinates. This vector has an x-coordinate of `z` and a y-coordinate of `y`.

usage.description: This code creates a new instance of the Vector3f class with the values 2, 4, and 8 for x, y, and z respectively. Then it uses the getZY() method to create a Vector2f object containing only the z and y components of the vector. Finally, it prints out the string representation of the Vector2f object which is "Z and Y components of the vector are: (8 4)".

items.name: getZY

items.description: returns a new `Vector2f` object containing the values of `x` and `z`.

returns.description:
a `Vector2f` object containing the `x` and `z` coordinates of the point.

The Vector2f object returned is defined by two components, `x` and `z`, which represent the x-coordinate and z-coordinate of the point, respectively.

usage.code:
Vector3f v = new Vector3f(1, 2, 3);
Vector2f xz = v.getXZ(); // xz is now (1, 3)

items.name: getXZ

items.description: sets the x, y, and z components of a `Vector3f` object to the input values, returning a reference to the modified object.

params.name: x
params.description: 3D position of the vector along the x-axis.

params.name: y
params.description: 2D position of the vector in the Y-axis direction, and its value is used to update the corresponding component of the vector.

params.name: z
params.description: 3D position of the vector in the z-axis, and its value is assigned to the `z` field of the `Vector3f` object.

returns.description:
a reference to the same `Vector3f` instance with the updated x, y, and z values.

* The output is a reference to the same `Vector3f` instance as the input parameters.
* The `x`, `y`, and `z` fields of the output object are assigned the values of the corresponding input parameters.
* The output object retains its original identity, meaning it can be used in further method calls or operations without any changes to its state.

usage.code:
Vector3f myVector = new Vector3f(0, 0, 0);
myVector.set(1, 2, 3).rotate(new Vector3f(0, 0, 1), 45).normalized().add(new Vector3f(4, 6, 7)).sub(new Vector3f(8, 9, 10));
usage.description: The code above first creates a new vector myVector with zero values. Then it uses the set method to change the x, y and z values of the vector to (1, 2, 3). Then it rotates the vector by 45 degrees around the axis specified in Vector3f(0, 0, 1). Then it normalizes the vector and adds the vector Vector3f(4, 6, 7) to it. Finally, it subtracts Vector3f(8, 9, 10) from it.

items.name: set

items.description: sets the components of a `Vector3f` object to the corresponding values of another `Vector3f` object.

params.name: r
params.description:
3D vector that contains the new values for the object's position, and it is used to set the object's position by passing its components directly into the `set()` method.

`r.getX()`: The x-coordinate of the vector.

`r.getY()`: The y-coordinate of the vector.

`r.getZ()`: The z-coordinate of the vector.

returns.description:
a reference to the same `Vector3f` object, which has its components set to the values provided in the input parameter.

* The output is a reference to the same `Vector3f` instance as the input parameter `r`.
* The output contains the new values for the x, y, and z components of the vector, which are obtained by calling the `getX()`, `getY()`, and `getZ()` methods on the input parameter `r`.
* The output has the same properties and attributes as the input parameter `r`, including its position, direction, or any other attributes defined within the `Vector3f` class.

items.name: set

items.description: returns the value of the `x` field.

returns.description: a floating-point representation of the variable `x`.

usage.code: float x = vector3f.getX();

items.name: getX

items.description: sets the value of the `x` field of the object it is called on to the input parameter `x`.

params.name: x
params.description: float value that will be assigned to the `x` field of the class instance being manipulated by the function.

items.name: setX

items.description: retrieves the value of a `y` field, which is assumed to be a `float`. It returns the stored value.

returns.description: the value of the `y` field.

usage.description: This code creates a new instance of the Vector3f class and sets its coordinates using the set method. Then it retrieves the y coordinate using the getY method and prints it to the console. The output would be "2".

items.name: getY

items.description: sets the value of the `y` field within the object to which it belongs.

params.name: y
params.description: 2-dimensional coordinate value for setting the Y component of an object's location.

usage.description: In this example, we create a new instance of the Vector3f class and then use the setY method to set the value of the y component to 5.0f.

items.name: setY

items.description: retrieves the value of the `z` field, which represents an object's position along the z-axis.

returns.description: the value of the `z` field.

items.name: getZ

items.description: sets the value of the `z` field of its object argument to the inputted float value.

params.name: z
params.description: 3D position of an object in the coordinate system and assigns it to the field `z` of the class, which stores the 3D position of the object.

items.name: setZ

items.description: compares a `Vector3f` object with another one, returning `true` if all its components are equal, and `false` otherwise.

params.name: r
params.description:
3D vector to compare with the current object's 3D coordinates.

* `x`: represents the x-coordinate of the vector
* `y`: represents the y-coordinate of the vector
* `z`: represents the z-coordinate of the vector

returns.description: a boolean value indicating whether the vector's components are equal to those of the provided vector.

usage.description: The method equals compares two vectors and determines if they are equal based on their X, Y, and Z components. In this example, the vectors are not equal because their X, Y, and Z components do not match.

structured.description: A class `Block` that represents a 3D voxel in a grid. The class has several instance variables for storing information about the position and state of the voxel, including `x`, `y`, and `z` coordinates and boolean flags for various properties such as `ft`, `bk`, `tp`, `bt`, `lt`, and `rt`. These properties can be initialized or modified using constructors and methods provided by the class.

items.description:
represents a three-dimensional block with x, y, and z coordinates and various boolean flags for its properties.
Fields:
	- z (int): represents the depth value of the block in the 3D space.
	- rt (boolean): represents whether the block has been right-clicked on by a player.

fields.name: z
fields.description: represents the depth value of the block in the 3D space.

fields.name: rt
fields.description: represents whether the block has been right-clicked on by a player.

items.name: Block

structured.description: A 3D rendering engine for a blocky terrain, using the Chunk class as the basic rendering unit. The Chunk class represents a 3D cube with an ID and a color, and has methods for updating its blocks and rendering itself. The engine renders the chunks in a 2D plane by iterating over them in a nested loop and applying a shader to each chunk based on its position. The shader uniforms are set using the render method, which also applies the view-projection matrix to each chunk's model matrix for proper perspective-correct rendering.

items.description: in the provided code is a 3D rendering engine that generates and renders chunks of a 3D world based on user input. The World class has several fields and methods related to chunk generation, rendering, and management. The main method render(Shader s, Camera c) is responsible for rendering the chunks in the 3D world from the user's perspective.
items.name: World

items.description: iterates over a 3D grid of chunks, creating new chunks at each position and updating their blocks and transforming them into a gen model.

usage.description: This code creates a new chunk at the given x, y and z coordinates, updates its blocks to match those in the world, and then converts it into a model that can be rendered. This method is called whenever a new chunk needs to be generated.

items.name: gen

items.description: updates the position of a `World` object based on user input, checking for collisions with existing chunks and adjusting the position accordingly.

params.name: x
params.description: 2D coordinate of the chunk being updated, and is used to determine which chunks are affected by the update operation.

params.name: y
params.description: 2D coordinate of the chunk's position within the Minecraft world, and is used to determine whether the chunk has been updated or not based on its distance from the current position of the `World` object.

params.name: z
params.description: 3D coordinate of the chunk's position in the world, and it is used to determine whether the chunk is within the specified range of coordinates for the generation process.

usage.description: Note that this method is not intended for use by the user and is used solely for internal implementation of the class. Therefore, it does not have any access modifiers or parameters. It also has a return type of void since it does not return anything.

items.name: updatePos

items.description: renders a 3D model using a shader and a camera. It iterates over each pixel of the model, applying the shader's uniforms and drawing the model for each pixel.

params.name: s
params.description:
3D rendering shader that the method `render()` is applying its effects to.

* `s`: A `Shader` object representing a shading program for rendering 3D objects.
* `c`: A `Camera` object used to determine the view and projection matrices for rendering.

params.name: c
params.description:
3D rendering camera, which is used to transform the 3D scene coordinates into screen space coordinates for rendering.

* `Camera c`: This is an instance of the `Camera` class, which represents a camera in 3D space. It has various properties and attributes, such as its position, orientation, and field of view.
* `W`, `H`, and `D`: These are constants that represent the width, height, and depth of the chunky array `chunks`.
* `Chunk ch`: This is an instance of the `Chunk` class, which represents a 3D cube in the scene. It has various properties and attributes, such as its position, color, and model matrix.

usage.description:
Explanation:
The first line creates a shader object named `s`. The second line creates a camera object named `c`. The third line creates a world object named `w` at position (0, 0, 0). The fourth line generates the chunks of the world. The fifth line uses the shader object `s`. Finally, the sixth line renders the world using the camera and shader objects.
This example is short and should work correctly. However, it does not explain why this code works or what each part does. Therefore, it is not recommended to use it as a unit test for the render method.

items.name: render
